title: Curried goat
subtitle: Jamaican goat or lamb slow-cooked in Scotch bonnet curry sauce
excerpt: Bone-in goat or lamb cooked low and slow in a sauce built on Jamaican curry powder, Scotch bonnet, allspice, and thyme until the meat is falling from the bone. A cornerstone of Jamaican cooking and a dish that requires nothing beyond time. Scotch bonnet gives more flavour than just heat, keep it whole for a milder result.
sourceNotes: Curried goat is a cornerstone of Jamaican cooking, introduced through the Indian and South Asian communities who came to Jamaica as indentured labourers after the abolition of slavery. The dish combines South Asian curry technique with Jamaican ingredients (Scotch bonnet, allspice) and is documented in Jamaican household cookery accounts from the nineteenth century. Multiple cross-referenced Caribbean and diaspora cookery sources.

content.text: Goat is the correct meat for this dish, cheaper, leaner, and more flavourful than lamb, with a firmer texture that holds up to the long braise. It is available from halal butchers and many supermarkets. If goat is not available, bone-in lamb shoulder on the bone works and gives a richer result. The bone is essential for the depth it adds to the sauce.

The Scotch bonnet is the most distinctive ingredient. It provides not only heat but a specific fruity, floral flavour that is the signature of Caribbean cooking and cannot be replicated by other chillies. Adding it whole rather than chopped keeps most of the heat in check, the flavour releases gently into the sauce without the full capsaicin impact of a chopped chilli. If you prefer more heat, chop it finely and add it with the onions.

items.name: Lamb shoulder
items.prepNote: bone-in goat, or lamb shoulder on the bone, cut into 5 cm pieces

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: left whole for moderate heat, or finely chopped for more

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of oil in a dutch oven over high heat. Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} of meat with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and brown in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onion to the pot and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-ground}} of ginger, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme, and {{curry-powder}} of curry powder. Stir for 2 minutes. Add the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and stir for 1 minute. Return the meat to the pot.

Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of stock. Add the {{scotch-bonnet}} Scotch bonnet whole. Bring to the boil, cover, and simmer over low heat for 1 hour 30 minutes until the meat is very tender and pulling from the bone. Remove the Scotch bonnet. Remove the lid and simmer for 15 minutes to thicken the sauce. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve over rice, scattered with {{spring-onion}} spring onions.

items.fix: The simmer must be very gentle; add 30 more minutes covered over low heat; goat takes longer than lamb
items.cause: Heat too high, causing the meat to tighten rather than braise

items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer uncovered for 20 minutes to reduce; the sauce should coat the back of a spoon
items.cause: Too much stock, or lid not removed at the end

items.fix: For more heat, chop the Scotch bonnet finely and fry with the onions; the whole bonnet gives fragrance without full heat
items.cause: Scotch bonnet left intact, most heat stays in the flesh and seeds
items.symptom: Dish is not hot enough despite using a whole Scotch bonnet

content.text: Curried goat came to Jamaica with Indian indentured labourers after the abolition of slavery in 1838. They brought the slow braise and the spiced masala sauce. In Jamaica they combined those techniques with local ingredients: Scotch bonnet, allspice, and goat. Allspice grows almost nowhere else. Goat had been on the island since the Spanish arrived in the 1500s. The dish that resulted belongs entirely to Jamaica. It has been a fixture of Jamaican home cooking for nearly two centuries.
